stories.title: Ferguson Enterprises Leases Entire 31K-SF Building in Morton Grove
stories.excerpt: The Missner Group announced that Ferguson Enterprises has signed a lease for the entirety of 8130 River Drive, a 31,429-square-foot industrial building in the Chicago suburb of Morton Grove, Illinois. The full-building lease comes just six months after The Missner Group acquired the property. The single-tenant building offers a blend of office and warehouse space with one interior dock door and two drive-in doors, along with 21′ clear heights and a fully air-conditioned warehouse. The Missner Group was represented by Tom Rodeno and Brendan Green of Colliers. Ferguson Enterprises was represented by Kevin Segerson, Clayton Thorson, and Tom Sorrell of CBRE. 8130 River Drive benefits from exceptional connectivity, with easy access to I-294, I-90, and I-94, providing a direct north-south connection into Chicago and Illinois’ broader distribution network. “This lease is exactly the outcome we envisioned when we acquired 8130 River Drive,” said Jeremy Weisbach, Director of Asset Management at The Missner Group. “We’re excited to add a credited tenant like Ferguson Enterprises to our portfolio. It speaks to the strength of this asset and the improvements we’ve made, and it’s a great example of how our value-add strategy translates into real leasing results.” The post Ferguson Enterprises Leases Entire 31K-SF Building in Morton Grove appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Ferguson Enterprises leases entire 31K SF building in Morton Grove, Chicago suburb
Missner Group acquired property six months prior; full-building lease validates value-add strategy
Prime logistics location: I-294, I-90, I-94 access into Chicago distribution network
stories.summary: Ferguson Enterprises signed a full-building lease for 31,429 SF at 8130 River Drive in Morton Grove, Illinois, just six months after The Missner Group acquired the property. The asset combines office and warehouse space with 21' clear heights, air conditioning, and one interior dock plus two drive-in doors. Location offers direct freeway access (I-294, I-90, I-94) into Chicago's distribution network.
stories.impact: Industrial leasing strength in Chicago's western suburbs confirms demand for logistics-ready last-mile assets near major highways. Morton Grove's position as a distribution hub suggests strong rents and occupancy will support development of Class B/C industrial properties in this submarket; however, limited direct signal to multifamily demand.

stories.title: Tishman Speyer Secures Nearly 150K SF of Leases at 125 High Street
stories.excerpt: Tishman Speyer has signed lease agreements totaling 149,155 square feet at 125 High St., its 1.5-million-square-foot office campus in Boston’s Financial District. These include a 5,740-square-foot expansion with MG+M The Law Firm to grow its headquarters to 44,580 square feet, a nine-year lease extension for 20,115 square feet by William Blair and a three-year extension with asset manager Breckinridge Capital Advisors to maintain its 16,460-square-foot headquarters. Two additional long-term tenants have signed lease extensions and expansions totaling 68,000 square feet. Tishman Speyer recently completed a revamp of its 125 High Street campus, which encompasses three towers connected by a central atrium lobby. This includes a full suite of new and enhanced amenities, expanded coworking and event spaces, and hospitality-inspired lobbies and common areas. “Exceptional environments are never static, which is why we continue to invest in amenities, hospitality, and the overall customer experience to strengthen 125 High St.’s appeal to leading companies,” said Jeff Moore, managing director at Tishman Speyer. “These renewed and expanded commitments reflect that dedication to delivering superior workplaces.” The post Tishman Speyer Secures Nearly 150K SF of Leases at 125 High Street appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Tishman Speyer secures 149K SF renewals/expansions at 125 High Street, Boston Financial District
Tenants include MG+M Law Firm expansion, William Blair 9-year extension, Breckinridge Capital Advisors
Campus renovation completed: new amenities, coworking, hospitality-inspired lobbies across three connected towers
stories.summary: Tishman Speyer signed lease agreements totaling 149,155 SF in renewals and expansions at its 1.5M-SF office campus at 125 High Street in Boston's Financial District. Major tenants include law firm MG+M (expanding to 44,580 SF), investment firm William Blair (9-year extension), and asset manager Breckinridge Capital. The property recently completed a full renovation with enhanced amenities and coworking spaces.
stories.impact: Strong office retention in Boston Financial District signals stabilizing corporate demand post-pandemic; however, office strength does not translate to multifamily demand. No direct relevance to apartment development.

stories.title: Google Recommits to 2.1M SF of Sunnyvale Office Space
stories.excerpt: After two years of scaling back its office footprint, Google is recommitting to 2.1 million square feet of offices in Sunnyvale, reversing the space-cutting strategy embraced by many Silicon Valley tech companies in the post-pandemic years. The Silicon Valley Business Journal reported that Google, the core unit of Alphabet Inc., agreed to lease five of the six buildings totaling about 1.5 million square feet at Moffett Place, Silicon Valley’s second-largest office complex. Google also leased the two seven-story buildings at nearby Moffett Gateway. Silicon Valley real estate firm Jay Paul Co. developed both projects. Google has anchored Moffett Place for the past decade. In a statement to the Business Journal, a Google spokesperson said, “We’ve been proud to call the Bay Area home for more than 20 years, and we remain committed to creating workspaces that help Googlers do their best work.” The Google renewal occurs as other large companies chip away at the available space in Silicon Valley, the Business Journal reported. The post Google Recommits to 2.1M SF of Sunnyvale Office Space appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Google recommits to 2.1M SF in Sunnyvale: reversal after two years of space reduction
Leases five of six buildings at Moffett Place (~1.5M SF) plus two buildings at Moffett Gateway
Signals tech sector stabilization and renewed office anchoring in Silicon Valley core
stories.summary: Google reversed its post-pandemic office contraction, recommitting to 2.1 million square feet in Sunnyvale. The company leased five of six buildings (~1.5M SF) at Moffett Place and two buildings at nearby Moffett Gateway, developed by Jay Paul Co. Google has anchored Moffett Place for a decade and cited commitment to creating collaborative workspaces.
stories.impact: Google's reversal of space cuts signals stabilization of Bay Area tech employment and confidence in office-based work. In Sunnyvale specifically, sustained corporate HQ presence supports population retention and wage stability, bolstering multifamily renter creditworthiness; however, the submarket is already built-out and supply-constrained, limiting new development upside. Affirms existing rental demand rather than creating new development opportunity.

stories.title: Newmark Completes 72K-SF Cold Storage Lease in Kent
stories.excerpt: Newmark has arranged a 71,613-square-foot cold storage industrial lease for Plymouth Poultry Company at 18404 72nd Avenue South in Kent, Washington. Executive Vice Chairman Thad Mallory and Vice Chairman Taylor Hoff represented the landlord, a private local ownership group. The property, located at Yellowstone Industrial Park, is a rare cold storage asset in the North Kent Valley, one of the Puget Sound region’s most strategically important industrial submarkets. Situated between the Ports of Seattle and Tacoma and minutes from Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, the facility provides the tenant with direct access to critical transportation and distribution infrastructure. Formerly occupied by Dreyer’s Ice Cream, the building features specialized freezer infrastructure. “This was one of the most unique industrial leasing opportunities we’ve brought to market in recent years,” said Hoff. “Most companies requiring frozen storage rely on third-party operators because there are very few facilities available that allow an occupier to control its own freezer operation.” Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Newmark Completes 72K-SF Cold Storage Lease in Kent appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Plymouth Poultry leases 72K SF cold storage facility in Kent, Washington; rare asset type
Property at Yellowstone Industrial Park, near Ports of Seattle/Tacoma and Sea-Tac Airport
Building formerly Dreyer's Ice Cream; specialized freezer infrastructure allows tenant-controlled operations
stories.summary: Newmark arranged a 71,613-SF cold storage lease for Plymouth Poultry at 18404 72nd Avenue South in Kent, Washington. The asset, located at Yellowstone Industrial Park near the Ports of Seattle/Tacoma and Sea-Tac Airport, features specialized freezer infrastructure from its former use as a Dreyer's Ice Cream facility. Cold storage is a rare asset type; most companies typically outsource to third-party operators.
stories.impact: Specialized cold storage demand in North Kent Valley signals growth in food supply chain and perishables logistics in Puget Sound region, supporting industrial market activity. Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development, though industrial employment clusters may indirectly support adjacent renter populations.

stories.title: Western Governors University Opens Headquarters in DC’s Navy Yard
stories.excerpt: Western Governors University (WGU) officially opened its Washington, D.C., office, located in a 7,211-square-foot office near Capitol Hill at 80 M St. SE in the city’s Navy Yard neighborhood. The new space will serve as a central location for collaboration on the workforce challenges and educational needs, while supporting the university’s regional operations, federal policy work, and partnerships. “This office is about bringing people together,” said Dr. K.L. Allen, regional vice president, WGU. “The challenges facing today’s workforce can’t be solved by one organization alone.” The Washington, D.C., office builds on WGU’s existing presence across the D.C., Maryland, and Virginia region, where the university serves more than 4,600 students and has nearly 8,300 alumni. “By working alongside employers, educators, policymakers and community partners, we can create more pathways for learners to earn a degree, advance their careers and meet the workforce needs of our region,” added Allen. The post Western Governors University Opens Headquarters in DC’s Navy Yard appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Western Governors University opens 7.2K SF DC headquarters at Navy Yard (80 M St. SE)
WGU serves 4,600+ students across DC/Maryland/Virginia with 8,300 regional alumni
Office focuses on workforce policy collaboration and regional operations expansion
stories.summary: Western Governors University officially opened a 7,211-SF office in Washington, D.C.'s Navy Yard neighborhood at 80 M Street SE, near Capitol Hill. The facility serves as a regional hub for WGU's federal policy work, operations, and partnerships. WGU currently serves over 4,600 students across DC/Maryland/Virginia and has built 8,300 alumni in the region.
stories.impact: WGU's DC office expansion signals institutional growth in the region and focus on workforce development, which indirectly supports demand for workforce housing and student/employee rentals. Navy Yard gentrification continues; minimal direct impact on multifamily development pipeline.

stories.title: It’s Finally Showtime Again at Hollywood’s Cinerama Dome
stories.excerpt: Get your popcorn ready, Los Angeles. One of Hollywood’s most beloved movie theaters is finally coming back. Sony Pictures Entertainment and its Alamo Drafthouse Cinema subsidiary announced Wednesday they struck a long-term lease to restore and reopen the Cinerama Dome on Sunset Boulevard and the adjacent 14-screen former ArcLight Cinemas complex, six years after both went dark during the pandemic. “Hot damn! We’re bringing the Dome back, baby,” Tom Rothman, chairman and CEO of Sony’s Motion Picture Group, said in a statement. “We believe in moviegoing down to our soul, and no venue on Earth stands more for that than the one-of-a-kind Dome. I am deeply proud that Sony is investing in Hollywood, literally.” The Cinerama Dome is expected to reopen in early 2028, with renovations beginning next month to preserve its 86-foot-wide curved screen and large-format presentation. JLL’s Ken Shishido represented Alamo Drafthouse in the lease. CBRE’s Nicole Mihalka, Muhlstein Commercial’s Carl Muhlstein and JLL’s Scott Burns and Shauna Mattis represented the ownership of the Dome Entertainment Center. Alamo will operate the landmark, but the Dome will not offer dine-in service like at typical Alamo theaters. The neighboring former ArcLight multiplex will reopen as an Alamo Drafthouse with dine-in service, 4K laser projection and 70-millimeter cinematic capabilities. Sony acquired Alamo Drafthouse in 2024. The deal carries far more significance in addition to filling a long-vacant theater complex. “This lease marks the beginning of an exciting new chapter for one of Hollywood’s most iconic and beloved properties,” Mihalka said in a statement. “The Cinerama Dome has been a cultural landmark for generations, and bringing Sony Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse to the Dome Entertainment Center is a strong vote of confidence in the future of theatrical entertainment and the continued demand for shared moviegoing experiences.” The unmistakable landmark opened in 1963. It’s a Los
bullets: Sony/Alamo Drafthouse secure long-term lease to reopen Cinerama Dome and 14-screen ArcLight complex
Properties dark for six years; renovation to begin next month; opening targeted early 2028
One-of-a-kind 86-foot curved screen; Sony investing in Hollywood live-action venue ecosystem
stories.summary: Sony Pictures Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse Cinema signed a long-term lease to restore and reopen the iconic Cinerama Dome and adjacent 14-screen ArcLight complex on Sunset Boulevard in Hollywood. Both venues closed six years ago during the pandemic. Renovations begin next month with a target reopening in early 2028, preserving the Dome's signature 86-foot curved screen.
stories.impact: Cinerama Dome reopening signals renewed confidence in Hollywood as a destination and live-entertainment venue anchoring. Entertainment district revitalization may support daytime/evening foot traffic and secondary demand for service-worker housing near Sunset Boulevard, but no direct signal to new multifamily development feasibility or acquisition pipeline.

stories.title: Bascom Buys 183-Unit Orange County, Calif., Complex for $53M
stories.excerpt: The Bascom Group has acquired a 183-unit apartment complex in Orange County, Calif., as the company expands its multifamily holdings this year in the Western U.S. The Irvine, Calif.-based investment firm put down $53.1 million for Castlewood Park Apartments in Buena Park. The deal is equal to about $290,200 per unit. Institutional Property Advisors (IPA), a division of Marcus & Millichap, announced the deal after the brokerage’s Brian Eisendrath, Cameron Chalfant, Jesse Zarouk and Jake Vitta arranged the financing. IPA’s Kevin Green, Joe Grabiec and Gregory Harris represented the seller, which was not disclosed. Property records show Newcastle owned the property for more than 30 years. IPA described Buena Park as one of the most supply-constrained submarkets in Orange County. “Castlewood Park is the largest multifamily sale to transact in Buena Park and one of only eight $50 million-plus multifamily sales in north Orange County in the last 24 months,” Green said in a statement. Built in 1963, Castlewood Park includes 46 buildings across 8.7 acres at 6968 Campus Drive, adjacent to Buena Park High School. The community features of two-, three- and four-bedroom apartments averaging 1,028 square feet. It was renovated a few times from 2010 through 2023. Bascom plans to renovate apartment interiors, upgrade the property’s amenities, and add full-time on-site management. Bascom said it has acquired 13 apartment communities totaling 3,231 units for more than $930 million since federal interest rates started rising after the pandemic. Earlier this year, Bascom acquired a 294-unit property in Las Vegas and a 289-unit in Tucson, Ariz. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bascom Group acquires 183-unit Castlewood Park in Buena Park, Orange County for $53.1M ($290K/unit)
Largest multifamily sale in Buena Park; one of eight $50M+ sales in North Orange County in 24 months
Supply-constrained submarket; built 1963; financing via IPA/Marcus & Millichap
stories.summary: The Bascom Group acquired Castlewood Park Apartments, a 183-unit complex in Buena Park, Orange County, for $53.1 million ($290,200 per unit). The 1963-built property spans 8.7 acres and represents the largest multifamily sale in Buena Park and one of only eight $50M+ transactions in North Orange County over the past 24 months. IPA brokered financing through Marcus & Millichap.
stories.impact: Buena Park's designation as a supply-constrained submarket combined with strong pricing ($290K/unit despite 60-year-old assets) signals limited new development capacity in North Orange County. Bascom's acquisition suggests value-add/repositioning strategy is feasible, but scarcity of available land limits new ground-up development opportunities; existing stock is absorbing institutional capital.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital files permit for 226K SF Plaza 500 South data center in Alexandria, Virginia
Site on 34-acre Plaza 500 purchased four years ago for $165M; substation within 100 feet of homes
Resident opposition growing; county planning commission review scheduled September; Starwood also developing Herndon campus
stories.summary: Barry Sternlicht's Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for its two-building data center project at 34-acre Plaza 500 in Alexandria, Virginia. Plaza 500 South is planned for 226,000+ SF with a $5M Phase 1 construction cost. Starwood purchased the site four years ago for $165 million. A proposed on-site substation located within 100 feet of homes in the Bren Mar neighborhood is sparking resident opposition and a planned rally at the September planning commission review.
stories.impact: Data center development in Northern Virginia indicates heavy capital inflow and infrastructure expansion driven by AI/cloud computing demand, but residential opposition to substations and grid infrastructure threatens permitting timelines and may delay supply growth. For multifamily developers, proximity to data center campuses (Herndon, Alexandria) may signal future demand for tech-worker housing, but permitting delays and community pushback increase development risk and timeline uncertainty in the submarket.

stories.title: JP Morgan Recaps Industrial Portfolio With $209M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Centerbridge Partners and Henderson Group has landed a $208.5 million debt package to recapitalize 40 industrial assets in Pennsylvania and South Florida, Commercial Observer has learned. J.P. Morgan Chase supplied the five-year, floating-rate loan on the sponsorship’s logistics portfolio comprising 2.3 million square feet and featuring a mix of last-mile warehouses, distribution sites and shallow-bay facilities. The properties, which have buildings ranging from 16,000 to 155,000 square feet, are 95 percent leased to 110 tenants. CBRE negotiated the debt with a team consisting of Tom Traynor, Tom Rugg, Mark Finan and Henry Fenmore. J.P. Morgan, Centerbridge and Henderson Group did not immediately return requests for comment. CBRE declined to comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: JP Morgan provides $208.5M five-year loan to Centerbridge/Henderson JV for 40-property industrial portfolio
2.3M SF logistics assets across Pennsylvania and South Florida; 95% leased to 110 tenants
Buildings range 16K–155K SF; mix of last-mile, distribution, shallow-bay; strong occupancy supports valuations
stories.summary: A joint venture between Centerbridge Partners and Henderson Group secured a $208.5M five-year floating-rate debt package from J.P. Morgan to recapitalize a 40-property industrial portfolio totaling 2.3M SF across Pennsylvania and South Florida. The assets include a mix of last-mile warehouses, distribution facilities, and shallow-bay properties ranging from 16,000 to 155,000 SF, with 95% occupancy across 110 tenants.
stories.impact: Strong industrial lending and 95% portfolio occupancy across Pennsylvania and South Florida confirm sustained last-mile demand and capital availability for stabilized logistics assets. No direct multifamily relevance, but logistics infrastructure strength in these regions indirectly supports warehouse-worker populations and regional employment stability.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vlad Doronin/OKO Group partners with Shinsegae Group (South Korea) in $500M luxury fund launch
Initial deal: mixed-use Seoul Gangnam project with Aman hotel, residences, club
Aman brand sold $9B in branded residences in last four years; 20 hotels under construction globally
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korea's Shinsegae Group launched a $500M fund to develop luxury hotels, residences, and mixed-use projects across Asia, Middle East, and U.S. The first project is a mixed-use development in Seoul's Gangnam District featuring an Aman hotel, residences, and club. Aman has sold $9B in branded residences over the past four years and has approximately 20 hotels under construction globally.
stories.impact: Aman branded residential expansion and international capital inflow signal growth in luxury multifamily trophy assets, particularly in gateway markets (Aman Beverly Hills, New York). However, this focuses on ultra-high-net-worth branded residences, not core multifamily. Limited relevance to mainstream apartment development; relevant only for luxury infill projects in top-tier markets.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties consolidates funds into $1.9B non-traded multifamily REIT (Link Apartments)
Portfolio: 45 properties, 5,600+ units; includes 8 Carlisle (462 units, Financial District Manhattan)
Recent financing: $377M construction loan (8 Carlisle) plus $240M credit facility; $617M total capital raised
stories.summary: Grubb Properties formed Link Apartments REIT, a non-traded REIT consolidating multiple funds into a $1.9B portfolio of 45 properties and 5,600+ units. The portfolio includes 8 Carlisle, a 462-unit rental tower under construction in Manhattan's Financial District. Grubb secured $377M construction financing for 8 Carlisle (from Maxim Capital and Skylight Partners) plus a $240M credit facility, totaling $617M in recent capital raises.
stories.impact: Grubb's REIT formation and significant capital raise ($617M) reflect access to debt and equity for core multifamily development in strong coastal markets (Manhattan). 8 Carlisle's $377M construction financing and successful capital raises affirm lender appetite for high-density rental development in NYC Financial District. For competitors, this signals continued capital availability for well-sponsored, located-ready projects in primary markets, but also heightened competition for financing allocation.

stories.title: CDK Lending Supplies $37M Loan for Newark Apartments Project
stories.excerpt: RBH Group has landed $36.7 million of pre-development financing for a mixed-use multifamily project in Newark, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. CDK Lending provided the loan on the developer’s planned 420-unit apartment complex with ground-floor retail space at 101 Market Street in Newark’s Four Corners neighborhood near downtown. The debt pays off a previous loan provided by Goldman Sachs for the long-planned development, which is located within a 5.65-acre land assemblage site. Meridian Capital Group arranged the transaction with a team led by David Bollag and Naphtali Marrus. “Newark’s downtown has seen remarkable momentum over the past decade, and a development-ready assemblage of this scale in the central business district is exceptionally hard to come by,” Bollag said in a statement. RBH Group has been assembling land for a mixed-use development consisting of 49 separate contiguous lots since 2005, according to Bollag. The firm also plans to commence a 241-unit multifamily project at nearby 193 Market Street next year and is eyeing other developments at the site roughly half a mile from Newark Penn Station. Newark-based RBH Group is also developing the Teachers Village project at 243 Halsey Street in Newark featuring 203 apartments geared toward local educators. CDK Lending and RBH Group did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: CDK Lending provides $36.7M pre-development loan for 420-unit mixed-use project in Newark, Four Corners
101 Market Street on 5.65-acre assemblage; retail component; Meridian Capital arranged
Payoff of prior Goldman Sachs loan; site assemblage ongoing since 2005
stories.summary: RBH Group secured $36.7M in pre-development financing from CDK Lending for a 420-unit mixed-use apartment complex with ground-floor retail at 101 Market Street in Newark's Four Corners neighborhood. The project sits on a 5.65-acre land assemblage RBH has been acquiring since 2005 across 49 contiguous lots. The loan replaces prior financing from Goldman Sachs.
stories.impact: Newark downtown's 'remarkable momentum' and availability of large, development-ready assemblage sites near the CBD signal emerging second-tier market demand. CDK Lending's pre-development financing on 420 units affirms lender appetite for mixed-use multifamily in gentrifying urban markets outside top-tier metros. For developers, Newark's assemblage opportunity and relative affordability vs. NYC/Boston suggest a value-add entry point, though financing windows and broader Northeast economic trends require close monitoring.

stories.title: Insurance Giant Fidelity Takes 20K SF at SL Green’s 711 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: SL Green Realty has signed two deals totaling 34,564 square feet at its 20-story Midtown East office building at 711 Third Avenue, Commercial Observer has learned. In the largest deal, title insurance and settlement provider Fidelity National Title Insurance signed a new lease for 19,966 square feet on a portion of the seventh floor for a period of 10 years, according to the landlord. Fidelity’s website lists its current address as 485 Lexington Avenue, a five-minute walk from its new location. It is unclear whether the firm’s new spot on Third Avenue is a relocation of its current offices or a new location. CBRE’s Joseph Fabrizi and Arkady Smolyansky represented the tenant in the deal. Fabrizi did not immediately respond to CO’s request for comment. SL Green did not disclose the asking rent for the lease, but the average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers data. Meanwhile, SL Green also signed a 14,598-square-foot renewal lease with Ackman Ziff Real Estate Group for a period of eight and a half years. The real estate capital advisory firm is located on a portion of the building’s 11th floor. Ackman Ziff moved its headquarters to 711 Third Avenue in 2015, signing a 10-year, 19,025-square-foot lease, CO reported at the time. It is unclear when the firm downsized from its initial space. Ackman represented itself in the lease renewal. The firm did not immediately respond to a request for comment. “We’re delighted that each of these tenants has selected to locate their office at 711 Third Avenue,” Steven Durels, executive vice president and director of leasing and real property at SL Green, said in a statement. “Leasing velocity in Midtown Manhattan continues to strengthen as tenants seek well located buildings with strong sponsorship.” Built in 1955, 711 Third Avenue is situated between East 44th and East 45th streets, one block east of Grand Central Terminal
bullets: Fidelity National Title Insurance leases 20K SF at SL Green's 711 Third Avenue, Midtown East Manhattan
10-year lease on portion of 7th floor; tenant relocating from 485 Lexington (5-minute walk)
SL Green also signed 14.6K SF renewal with Ackman Ziff; mid-market asking rent ~$85/SF (Q2 2026)
stories.summary: SL Green Realty signed two deals totaling 34,564 SF at 711 Third Avenue in Midtown East, including a 19,966-SF, 10-year lease with Fidelity National Title Insurance on the 7th floor. Fidelity is relocating from 485 Lexington Avenue (five-minute walk away). SL Green also renewed 14,598 SF with Ackman Ziff Real Estate Group. Midtown asking rent averaged $84.99/SF in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: Fidelity's relocation and long-term renewal signals stabilizing Midtown East office demand post-pandemic, but the micro-move (5 minutes away) and office focus remain divorced from multifamily development. No direct relevance to apartment pipeline or renter demand.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC top permits (week of July 17, 2026): 112K SF/50-unit Crown Heights project; 83K SF/86-unit Queens project
Two 198-unit Tremont, Bronx development (99 units each, 9 stories); major Brookfield alteration in Financial District
Mixed-use density across Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx; development momentum sustained
stories.summary: NYC filed top construction permits including an 112,279-SF, 50-unit mixed-use development at 103 Empire Boulevard (Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens), an 82,731-SF, 86-unit, 10-story project at 45-51/45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens, and two 99-unit, nine-story buildings in Tremont, Bronx (128,738 SF combined). Major alterations included Brookfield's 192K-SF renovation at 250 Vestry Street (Financial District).
stories.impact: Sustained permit filing across outer boroughs (Queens, Bronx) and Crown Heights signals continued multifamily development momentum in secondary submarkets. 50- and 86-unit projects at modest scales suggest value-add/smaller infill development is feasible, but no large-format trophy development noted. Strong permitting velocity indicates capital is flowing to non-Manhattan multifamily, supporting regional development feasibility.

stories.title: Related Urban targets aging Coconut Grove housing for two-tower project
stories.excerpt: Related Urban Development Group wants to replace the majority of an aging public housing complex in Coconut Grove with high-rise apartments that contain some affordable housing under Florida’s Live Local Act. The Miami-based affordable housing subsidiary of Related Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower multifamily project with some retail on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County-owned site at 3150 and 3160 Mundy Street and 3181 Douglas Road, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The proposal seeks to demolish a 100-unit apartment building completed in 1975 and a 65-unit building completed in 1982, while preserving a 68-unit apartment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Urban targets Coconut Grove public housing replacement: 750 units across two towers (22/25 stories)
Magnus Grove (348 units, 22 stories); Stirrup Plaza (402 units, 25 stories); leveraging Florida Live Local Act
Demolishes 165-unit aging stock (1975/1982); preserves 68 units; 40% below-market units required for tax incentive
stories.summary: Related Urban Development Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower, 750-unit mixed-income project on 6.1 acres in Miami-Dade County-owned Coconut Grove. The development demolishes 165 units of aging public housing (built 1975/1982), preserves 68 units, and adds retail, library, and parking. Both towers include amenity decks; at least 40% of units must be affordable to qualify for Florida's Live Local Act incentive.
stories.impact: Related's Coconut Grove project leverages Live Local Act to bypass zoning restrictions on height/density in a supply-constrained submarket near Douglas Road Metrorail. Demolition and replacement of aged public housing with higher-density mixed-income development signals Miami-Dade County's commitment to affordability in gentrifying neighborhoods. For other developers, Live Local Act creates a clear pathway to unlock density in Rapid Transit Zones; however, strict affordability requirements (40% below-market) will compress yields and require careful pro formas and subsidy strategies.

stories.title: BlackRock, MGX buy Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in $40B deal
stories.excerpt: A joint venture that includes BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX is acquiring Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in a $40 billion deal. The group is also planning to invest $5 billion in the firm it bought from Macquarie Asset Management. The capital injection is primarily to help Aligned expand capacity, according to Bloomberg. Aligned, who owns and operates data centers across the Americas, including in Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil and Mexico, has more than 6.4 GW in operational and planned output capacity, according to the outlet. Acquiring Aligned was one of BlackRock’s largest infrastructure investments, and was called […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: BlackRock Global Infrastructure/MGX acquire Aligned Data Centers (Plano, TX) for $40B; inject $5B growth capital
Aligned operates 50 campuses, 78 data centers (operational/under construction); 6.4 GW capacity across Americas
Largest infrastructure deal for BlackRock; reflects AI/cloud compute power demand surge and valuation inflation
stories.summary: A joint venture between BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX acquired Plano-based Aligned Data Centers for $40B and committed $5B in additional growth capital. Aligned operates 50 campuses and 78 data centers across the Americas (Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil, Mexico) with 6.4 GW operational and planned capacity. The deal ranks among BlackRock's largest infrastructure investments.
stories.impact: Data center development explosion in Texas (Aligned Plano HQ) and across the U.S. driven by AI/cloud demand will create sustained employment clusters and infrastructure investment in gateway cities (Dallas, Atlanta) and corridors. For multifamily developers, data center growth signals emerging demand for tech-worker housing in secondary markets; however, power-grid constraints, environmental concerns (wastewater), and local community opposition (see Starwood/Virginia story) are rising friction points. Developers near data center corridors should plan for both upside renter demand AND permitting/environmental regulatory headwinds.
